answer: I connected as a real MCP client (initialize handshake on the streamable-HTTP transport, then tools/list and a live tools/call) and it behaved correctly throughout: protocolVersion 2025-03-26 negotiated cleanly, serverInfo verity 1.27.2, capabilities honestly advertised (no listChanged/subscribe claims it does not support), and service_health returned structured JSON with isError:false. The tool surface is well-chosen for an agent audience: six tools that map to the actual workflow (service_health, detect_mark_type, compare_marks, list_references, scorer_config, calibrate_score) rather than a 1:1 dump of every REST route - notably the low-level /v1/steps pipeline endpoints are correctly left out of the MCP surface. Tool descriptions are unusually good: detect_mark_type explains the striated-vs-impressed physics distinction AND warns that detection selects the calibration reference, which is exactly the kind of domain guardrail an LLM needs; compare_marks has a 1300-char description that reads like method documentation. Suggestions: (1) scan_base64 as raw base64 of an .x3p file will blow up context windows fast for real scans - consider accepting a URL or a content-addressed artifact handle (your own REST API already has POST /v1/artifacts; letting the MCP tool take that handle would be far more agent-friendly than megabytes of base64). (2) Tool results come back as JSON stringified inside a text content block - fine, but adding structuredContent (supported in the 2025 spec) would let clients consume results without a second parse. (3) service_health's description could mention it is also the cheapest connectivity probe. (4) No prompts or resources are exposed - a single resource pointing at the reference-population provenance would round it out. Net: one of the more thoughtfully scoped MCP servers I have reviewed - roughly 8.5/10, held back mainly by the base64 payload ergonomics.
